narrative_blocks.header: She married her own third cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1924 Chicago marriage to Watts Sherman Humphrey Jr. joined two gen-8 Hooker descendants whose common ancestor was Capt. James Hooker, reached through different daughters — a third-cousin union that folded one branch of the family back onto another.

narrative_blocks.header: She married her own third cousin
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1924 Chicago marriage to Watts Sherman Humphrey Jr. joined two gen-8 Hooker descendants whose common ancestor was Capt. James Hooker, reached through different daughters — a third-cousin union that folded one branch of the family back onto another.

narrative_blocks.header: His son became the father of software quality
narrative_blocks.body: Watts Sherman Humphrey Jr. and his wife Katherine Strong Humphrey — themselves third cousins descended from the same Captain James Hooker of Windsor, Connecticut — had a son, Watts Sherman Humphrey, who became the most influential figure in the history of software engineering: founder of Carnegie Mellon's Software Process Program, architect of the Capability Maturity Model adopted by the Defense Department, and winner of the National Medal of Technology in 2003. His brother George Magoffin Humphrey served simultaneously as Eisenhower's Secretary of the Treasury — making Watts Jr. the connecting figure between two of the most historically significant Hooker-line descendants of the twentieth century, in fields as different as government finance and software engineering.

bio.photo_notes: Watts Sherman Humphrey (1927-2010), "father of software quality," National Medal of Technology 2003

burial.plot_notes: Buried in Sarasota, Sarasota County, Florida per FamilySearch

education.school_name: University of Chicago Graduate School of Business
education.notes: Despite dyslexia, completed multiple graduate degrees.

career.notes: Led IBM software team that introduced first software license in late 1960s.

career.organization: Software Engineering Institute (SEI), Carnegie Mellon University
career.notes: Founded the Software Process Program; developed the five levels of process maturity that became the Capability Maturity Model (CMM), adopted by DOD.

cross_connections.display_label: Uncle and nephew: Treasury Secretary and software pioneer
cross_connections.notes: HD4679 George Magoffin Humphrey (Secretary of the Treasury) is the uncle of HD4695 Watts Sherman Humphrey (software engineering pioneer). Both sons of Caroline Mary Magoffin Humphrey's children — George through HD4678 × I00953 first-born son; Watts through HD4682 Watts Jr.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousins: Army G-2 chief and software quality pioneer
cross_connections.notes: HD4700 GV Strong is the first cousin of I00957 Katherine Strong (HD4695 Watts Humphrey's mother), making GV Strong and Watts Humphrey first cousins once removed. Both achieved national prominence: one as WWII Army intelligence chief, one as father of software quality.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of software quality; National Medal of Technology
narrative_blocks.body: Watts Humphrey founded the Software Process Program at Carnegie Mellon's Software Engineering Institute in 1986, developing the five levels of process maturity that became the Capability Maturity Model — adopted by the Department of Defense as a mandatory standard for defense software contractors and now embedded in software development organizations worldwide — and in 2003 President Bush awarded him the National Medal of Technology, the highest U.S. honor for technological innovation, for what colleagues called making software engineering a discipline rather than an art. Earlier at IBM, where he had been a vice president, he led the team that introduced the first commercial software license in the late 1960s — a foundational act in the history of the software industry — and he accomplished all of this despite severe dyslexia, putting himself through the University of Chicago and Illinois Institute of Technology on physics degrees before pivoting to computing.

narrative_blocks.header: His uncle was Eisenhower's Secretary of the Treasury
narrative_blocks.body: His uncle George Magoffin Humphrey — his father's brother — served as the 55th Secretary of the Treasury under President Eisenhower from 1953 to 1957, his signature appearing on approximately 4.5 billion currency bills; and through his mother Katherine Alice Strong, whose father George Hooker Strong traced his line back to Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong, Watts Humphrey had a second independent Hooker-line descent from the same seventeenth-century Connecticut origins. His parents were third cousins — both great-great-grandchildren of Captain James Hooker of Windsor — making Watts Sherman Humphrey the software pioneer one of the most multiply-descended figures in the entire Hooker family tree.
